title: Ghost Network
shortDescription: Long-Term APT Inside a Defence Contractor

briefing.incidentSummary:
A defence contractor discovered that classified project files had been quietly exfiltrated for 14 months.

The attacker lived inside the network — moving laterally, avoiding detection, mimicking normal traffic patterns.

This was not a smash-and-grab. This was a ghost: patient, methodical, invisible.

They made one mistake. In month 14, they accessed a canary file — a honeypot document placed specifically to detect insider threats.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify the APT operator inside the organisation

› Map 14 months of lateral movement

› Understand what data was taken and why

› Collect at least 4 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Nalini V

digitalFootprint.vpnCheck: Internal network — no anomaly on surface

suspects.profileNotes: Recruited by a foreign intelligence handler 16 months ago via a compromised academic contact. Used legitimate access to build a mental map of the network over 2 months before any exfiltration began. Exfiltrated via steganographic files embedded in routine maintenance reports. Triggered the canary file while searching for a specific missile guidance specification.

suspects.name: Kabir J
suspects.profileNotes: Joined 10 months ago. Has broad network access due to role. However network activity is entirely within expected patterns. No after-hours anomalies. Used as a red herring — his broad access makes him suspicious at first glance.

suspects.name: Meghna T
suspects.profileNotes: Conducts biannual audits. Only has access during audit periods — the exfiltration spanned 14 months with activity between audit windows, ruling her out.

suspects.name: Sunil D
suspects.profileNotes: No access to technical systems. Unaware of the network topology. Handles personnel records — not a viable APT operator.

items.label: Package 7 received. Quality satisfactory. Continue to priority target: guidance_spec_v3.
items.detail: Recovered from an encrypted dead drop channel found on Nalini V's personal device. References 7 exfiltration packages received — confirming an ongoing, directed operation. The 'guidance_spec_v3' request led directly to the canary file trigger.

items.label: The position is research-oriented. We can discuss compensation at your convenience. 16 months prior.
items.detail: Email recovered from Nalini V's personal account — initial contact from the intelligence handler posing as an academic recruiter. Timeline aligns with the start of her network reconnaissance phase.

items.label: ALERT: Canary file 'guidance_decoy_v3.pdf' accessed — user NALINI-WS at 11:32 PM.
items.detail: The automated canary system flagged access to the honeypot document. This is the event that opened the investigation. The file had no legitimate reason to be accessed — it existed only to identify insider threats.

items.label: The new network engineer joined 15 months ago and requested access to 3 systems outside his role in his first week. Flagged at the time but approved.
items.detail: The new network engineer's unusual early access requests were flagged during the retrospective audit. He joined 1 month before Nalini V — the timing overlaps with the APT campaign start. However, his access requests were all to systems within the network engineering scope. His accounts show no access outside authorised systems, and no steganographic data was found in his documents.
items.irrelevantReason: The new engineer's access requests were within scope. Steganographic analysis found no hidden data in his documents. All lateral movement traces back to NALINI-WS.

items.label: steganography_analysis_report.pdf
items.detail: Digital forensics analysis of 14 maintenance reports authored by Nalini V over 14 months. Each report is a standard PDF — infrastructure inspection summaries, equipment health logs, and quarterly assessments. Steganographic analysis using LSB (least significant bit) extraction on embedded images revealed hidden data in 11 of the 14 reports. The hidden payloads range from 4KB to 22KB and are AES-encrypted. After key recovery (derived from a passphrase found in Nalini V's personal notes), the decrypted content includes: network topology diagrams, access credential lists for 6 internal systems, personnel security clearance levels, and fragments of project codenames. The reports were submitted through the normal document management system — giving them the appearance of routine paperwork while functioning as a covert data channel.

items.label: 14month_lateral_movement_map.pdf
items.detail: SIEM retrospective analysis covering 14 months of authentication logs across 312 internal systems. Nalini V's primary account (NALINI-WS) accessed 44 systems outside her documented role scope. Her legitimate access covers 6 systems in the infrastructure monitoring cluster. The 44 anomalous systems include: the HR personnel database (accessed 3 times), the project classification repository (accessed 11 times), the secure communications archive (accessed 7 times), and the network configuration vault (accessed 14 times). Each access lasted between 2 and 8 minutes — long enough to exfiltrate targeted files but short enough to avoid triggering session anomaly alerts tuned to 15-minute thresholds. Access events were distributed across different days and times to avoid pattern detection. Total data accessed across these sessions: approximately 2.1GB.

items.label: foreign_payment_trace.pdf
items.detail: Financial forensics report on secondary account activity linked to Nalini V. Monthly deposits of ₹2.2 Lakh were received every 4 weeks for 14 consecutive months — totalling ₹30.8 Lakh. The deposits originated from a layered chain: domestic transfer from a shell company 'DataSync Advisory LLP' → which received wire from a Singapore intermediary → which received SWIFT transfer from an account in Eastern Europe. DataSync Advisory LLP was registered 16 months ago — one month before the first deposit. Its registered address is a virtual office service in Chennai. Its only director is listed as a person who, per passport records, died in 2019 — a common identity fraud indicator. The payment cadence (every 28 days, always a weekend) is consistent with a handler-controlled disbursement schedule.

hiddenItem.label: handler_comms_analysis.txt
hiddenItem.detail: Linguistic and operational security analysis of 23 intercepted handler communications recovered from the dead-drop server. The communications use a codename-based system — Nalini V is referred to as 'SPARROW'. Linguistic analysis of the handler's messages identifies consistent stylistic markers: use of Oxford comma, specific abbreviation patterns, and a recurring phrase structure in tasking instructions. Cross-referencing with intelligence databases identified the communication style as consistent with a known handler designation from a foreign signals intelligence directorate. The operational security practices used — including the 28-day payment cycle and steganographic document channel — match the documented tradecraft of this directorate. Nalini V was likely recruited during an overseas conference she attended 16 months ago — 1 month before the first covert report was filed.

minigame.title: Analyse Handler Communication
minigame.emailBody:
SPARROW,

Monthly package received and verified. Disbursement of ₹2.2L initiated.

Next cycle: standard 28-day window. Priority targets: network topology update and Q4 personnel clearance list.

Maintain protocol. No deviations.

— Handler
redFlags: Codename 'SPARROW' matches the handler communication intercept
₹2.2L matches the exact monthly deposit amount
Sender domain is a shell company (metricsync-advisory.com)
minigame.hint: The codename, amount, and cadence match the financial forensics exactly.
hints: SPARROW is Nalini V's handler codename from the intercept.
₹2.2L every 28 days matches her secondary account deposits exactly.
This is handler-agent communication — report it immediately.

onSuccess.message: BREAKTHROUGH — handler_comms_analysis.txt confirmed. The handler's communication patterns match a known foreign intelligence directorate. Nalini V was a recruited agent for 14 months.

items.label: 14-Month Behavioural Baseline Deviation
items.detail: Nalini V's access patterns show a consistent 8% weekly drift from her established baseline — always toward higher-classification systems. Below the alert threshold individually, but cumulatively forming a directed reconnaissance path.

items.label: Report Publication Timing
items.detail: Each steganographic maintenance report was published within 48 hours of receiving a handler instruction. The correlation is 100% — every handler message was followed by a report within 2 days.

items.label: Canary File Access IP
items.detail: IP 10.10.5.44 accessed the canary file at 11:32 PM. This IP is permanently assigned to NALINI-WS. No shared workstation — this machine is exclusively used by Nalini V.

timeline.title: Recruitment Contact
timeline.description: Handler poses as academic recruiter — approaches Nalini V

timeline.title: Reconnaissance Begins
timeline.description: Nalini V starts mapping network — no exfiltration yet

timeline.title: Steganographic Exfiltration
timeline.description: 14 maintenance reports — 2.3GB hidden inside image metadata

timeline.title: Canary File Triggered
timeline.description: Nalini V accesses honeypot document searching for guidance spec

timeline.title: Alert Fires
timeline.description: Automated canary system notifies security team

perfect.title: Case Solved!
perfect.subtitle: Nalini V exposed as a long-term APT operator.
perfect.label: CASE CLOSED

partial.title: Insufficient Evidence
partial.subtitle: Correct suspect but the chain is incomplete.
partial.label: PARTIAL

wrongAccusation.title: Wrong Suspect
wrongAccusation.subtitle: The real culprit was Nalini V.
wrongAccusation.label: CASE FAILED

aria.welcomeToHub:
...14 months. 44 systems. 2.3 gigabytes. Not one alarm.
Then one wrong file. One canary. One mistake in 424 days.
That's who you're looking for.
aria.exploreFeeds:
Steganographic reports. Lateral movement maps. Financial traces.
This ghost left almost nothing. Almost.
aria.viewEvidence:
Every access under 3 minutes. Always during business hours. Always just below the threshold.
Patience like this is trained — not natural.
aria.markEvidence: Good. Every thread matters in a case this long.
aria.decryptionHint: The handler's contact is linked to a device. Decode the device owner.
aria.flagSuspect:
A trace is forming.
You're on to something — keep pulling the thread.
More fragments remain. Don't stop now.
